article_title: Trigona ransomware attacks use custom exfiltration tool to steal data
core_thesis: Trigona ransomware affiliates have developed a custom command-line data exfiltration tool, 'uploader_client.exe', to replace publicly available tools like Rclone and MegaSync, aiming to evade security detection and improve efficiency during double-extortion attacks. This shift indicates increased investment in proprietary malware to maintain a lower profile.
detailed_summary: Recent Trigona ransomware attacks observed by Symantec in March 2026 employed a custom exfiltration tool named 'uploader_client.exe'. This tool connects to a hardcoded server and features parallel uploads (five simultaneous connections per file), TCP connection rotation after 2GB of traffic, selective file type exfiltration (excluding large media files), and an authentication key to restrict access. In one incident, it stole high-value documents like invoices and PDFs from network drives. The attackers also used the Huorong Security Suite tool HRSword as a kernel driver, followed by utilities (PCHunter, Gmer, YDark, etc.) to disable security products via vulnerable kernel drivers. PowerRun was used for privilege escalation, AnyDesk for remote access, and Mimikatz/Nirsoft for credential theft. Trigona, a double-extortion ransomware group launched in October 2022, was disrupted in October 2023 by Ukrainian activists but has since resumed operations. Symantec provided IoCs for detection.

key_data_points: The custom exfiltration tool 'uploader_client.exe' supports five simultaneous connections per file for faster data theft (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: parallel uploads can be used to exfiltrate data quickly before detection).
The tool rotates TCP connections after 2GB of traffic to evade network monitoring (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: connection rotation helps bypass data volume thresholds and avoid triggering alerts).

Selective file type exfiltration excludes large, low-value media files, focusing on high-value documents like invoices and PDFs (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: targeting specific file types increases efficiency and reduces noise).

An authentication key is used to restrict access to stolen data, preventing other threat actors from accessing it (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this technique can be adopted to protect exfiltrated data from competitors).

Attackers used vulnerable kernel drivers to terminate endpoint protection processes, a technique known as 'Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver' (BYOVD) (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: BYOVD is a powerful method to disable EDR/AV; tools like PCHunter and Gmer can be leveraged).

PowerRun was used to launch executables with elevated privileges, bypassing user-mode protections (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: privilege escalation via PowerRun can be used to run payloads with SYSTEM integrity).

AnyDesk was used for persistent remote access, and Mimikatz/Nirsoft for credential theft (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: these are standard tools in red team toolkits; understanding their use in real attacks helps refine TTPs).
conclusions: Trigona's shift to a custom exfiltration tool demonstrates a trend among ransomware groups to develop proprietary malware to evade detection by security solutions that flag common tools like Rclone. For red teamers, this underscores the importance of custom tooling to avoid signature-based detection.

The use of BYOVD to disable security products is a critical technique that red teams should incorporate into their operations, as it effectively neutralizes endpoint defenses. Tools like PCHunter and Gmer are readily available and can be weaponized.

The combination of parallel uploads, TCP connection rotation, and selective file exfiltration in the custom tool provides a blueprint for efficient data theft. Red teams can adopt similar strategies to exfiltrate data quickly and stealthily during engagements.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming interests as it details real-world TTPs used by a ransomware group, including custom exfiltration tooling, BYOVD for defense evasion, and credential theft. You can directly apply these techniques in your own operations: develop or modify custom exfiltration tools with parallel uploads and connection rotation, use vulnerable drivers to kill EDR processes, and leverage tools like Mimikatz. The article also provides IoCs that can be used to test detection capabilities. As a programmer, you can analyze the tool's design (e.g., hardcoded server, authentication key) to build similar utilities for your red team engagements.
extra_advice: Consider building a custom exfiltration tool in C# or Go that implements parallel uploads, TCP connection rotation, and selective file filtering. Use the BYOVD technique with a vulnerable driver like 'gdrv.sys' to terminate security processes. Test these techniques in your homelab to refine your TTPs and ensure they work reliably before using them in engagements.
